diary: Friday felt like it had two lives. The morning was routine—coffee at home, the usual crawl down I-405 to the office in Tukwila. I spent most of the day buried in dashboards, trying to wrap up documentation before the weekend. Lunch at Southcenter was forgettable, but I needed the break. By 5 PM I was thinking about the weather, that perfect 77-degree breeze, and I remembered Capitol Hill Pride was kicking off tonight. I hadn't planned to go, but something shifted. I drove home, grabbed my camera on impulse, and suddenly I was heading back into the city as evening fell. Broadway was electric—strings of lights, music spilling from everywhere, people celebrating openly and joyfully. I wandered for nearly two hours just taking photos and absorbing it all. There's something about witnessing that kind of collective joy that reminds you why cities matter. Grabbed some food, talked to a few strangers, and drove home feeling lighter than I have in weeks.
thought_process: My day was split between obligation and spontaneity. Work was fine but unmemorable—the kind of Friday where you're just trying to finish strong and move on. But when I got home and saw the weather report, something clicked. I'm 57 and I live a pretty quiet life in Kirkland, and I realized I've been letting routine calcify me. The Pride event felt like permission to do something different, to be part of something bigger. I grabbed the camera almost as an excuse—gave myself a role, a reason to be there observing rather than just wandering. It made me feel less like an outsider. The drive back into the city after work felt like crossing into a different world. The energy downtown was infectious, and I think that's what I needed—a reminder that there's still spontaneity available to me if I reach for it.
world_event_prompt: Warm, breezy Friday, high of 77F. Seattle Pride kickoff: Capitol Hill street fair and road closures along Broadway and Pike from 5pm. Festive, busy evening downtown.
